data.title: ServiceNow: Enterprise Stickiness Will Not Be Threatened After AI Integration
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

ServiceNow, Inc. (NYSE: NOW) stock price has dropped over 50% over the last year driven by panic AI-related sell-off. However, I remain bullish on this company because the sentiment is an overblown one - The company has a lot of strategic moves, including the integration of Claude and OpenAI to optimize AI outcomes, that show AI is a core, functional layer within the ServiceNow ecosystem rather than a threat to its business.

From my point of view, the Company's fundamentals is resilient, given the intact financial performance that shows customer stickiness. This is in contrast of the sell-off which is triggered by worries about AI disruption and substitution. The company has managed to record a revenue growth rate YoY above 20% since 2021. At the end of FY2025, the company’s subscription revenue reached $12.9 billion. This is expected to improve to a revenue of $15.6 billion, which is a 21% YoY growth rate. The strong top-line growth is a testament to its value to its customers.

With current price returns already beginning to outperform the S&P 500, I believe this is an ideal entry point for an undervalued leader in enterprise automation. In the last month, this optimism has started manifesting in the company’s price return, which is beating the S&P 500. After the overshoot and sentiment, I believe this is where rational investors can come in.

AI Integration as a Growth Catalyst

ServiceNow. delivers cloud-based digital solutions globally. The company offers a wide range of IT services, risk, operations, workplace management, plus automation and AI-driven tools. These services serve varied industries and partners, especially those with autonomous AI. Since the company was incorporated in 2004, the Company has seen continuous growth in its customer base. Anyone in the IT industry knows, once an enterprise software is integrated into a company's system, it is hard and costly to replace it - this is where the stickiness comes in.

Let me dive in, but before that, let me first give a market picture that supports my perspective. One thing that is inseparable about cloud computing is that it is growing rapidly at a CAGR of 20.61% between 2026 and 2035. It is valued at $1.1 trillion today and is estimated to reach a market volume of $5.9 trillion by 2035.

Most of this growth is driven by task automation, which is decreasing human error, cutting costs, increasing productivity, and boosting efficiency, especially through cloud-based AI. For example, AI algorithms are working together with cloud computing to analyze and organize large data volumes to create useful blueprints. As a result, organisations are deriving value from this combination as it is offering flexible approaches, safe systems, facilitating adaptability, and enabling quick and economically strong AI systems. Therefore, AI is an augmentation to enterprise SaaS like ServiceNow by integrating advanced LLMs into its workflow, ServiceNow is enabling its customers to derive real economic value from their data and AI.

The best example is Deloitte, which has selected ServiceNow to modernize its IT service automation, reporting an 80% reduction in preparation time. Sales are benefitted by improving conversion rates, administration team is cutting time cost by almost 80%. This is a major productivity gain to Deloitte.

Some argues that with the rise of AI models, you might not need to subscribe to specific cloud computing services integrated with AI, because you can find that solution with other available AI models. While this might be true for individual users, AI systems are evolving to handle industry-specific routine tasks with precision inside a transparent and trustworthy framework. As a result, ServiceNow’s stickiness is from its ability to organize complex services into a single, trusted governance framework like Now Assist, making ServiceNow a "mission-critical" tool for organizations - this is where standalone AIs / LLMs cannot do.

In fact, when you look at the top business objectives in 2026, which are cost efficiency and workforce productivity - while risk reduction is also up in front. Therefore, when there is a service provider that can be accountable to risks and stability, companies are not willing to take this responsibility and work in house. This is exactly what ServiceNow is offering its customers with the recent AI partnerships, as long as the Company is still willing to keep up to the AI development and integration - this is the falsifiable condition to our thesis. Once ServiceNow is not considered as catching up and integrating the latest AI function integrated into the product suite, investors should review its investment into the company.

But until then, financial numbers reflect that customers are trusting the companies strongly.

Financials and Valuation

ServiceNow’s top-line performance aligns with my premises that its "stickiness" is stronger than ever. The company’s top-line trend has been on an uptrend sustainably. Since 2021, ServiceNow revenues has an over 20% YoY growth rate. Specifically, in 2025, total revenue reached $3.57 billion, while current Remaining Performance Obligations (cRPO) stand at $12.85 billion, up 25% year-over-year. This contrasts the sell-off.

Adjusted diluted EPS for Q4 is at $0.92, a 25% year-over-year increase that beat the consensus. Analysts expect FY2026 EPS to reach $4.18, which continues its double-digit growth.

When comparing valuations, ServiceNow is undervalued relative to its growth trajectory when its P/E multiple is measured against peers like Palo Alto Networks (PANW). Currently, NOW’s forward P/E ratio of 27.76x, which is lower than PANW’s 44.68x and Crowdstrike (CRWD)'s 91.03, while having comparable or even higher revenue growth.

In my view, this is an undervaluation. I believe NOW is more heavily impacted in this sell-off than its SaaS peers, which created an under-valuation opportunity. If valuation multiple converge and normalize, I think the company will have at least 20-30% higher share price when sentiment resumes, making it comparable to its top SaaS peers.

Investment Risk

  • Customer Cohort Concentration: ServiceNow has in the last 1 year has been maintaining around 98% customer renewal rate. However, I am seeing an underlying risk in over-reliance on deepening spend on existing customers as opposed to new customers. In Q3’2025, the renewal rate slipped to 97% but it has now recorded a rebound to 98% in Q4’2025. This is a testament to the fact that NOW is heavily relying on legacy customer contracts rather than new customer contracts. If the company fails to record new customers, it risks its future revenue sustainability
  • Failed to catch up to AI development: As discussed, my bullish thesis is based on the fact that ServiceNow is not being replaced by AI since it is adopting and integrating it. However, as Moore's Law goes, AI applications and ability is improving rapidly. If ServiceNow fails to follow the latest AI capability, it will be replaced.

Conclusion

I am reiterating a Buy rating on ServiceNow, as I think the sentiment during SaaS sell-off is unjustifiable and created an under-valuation opportunity in ths stock. Driven by the integration of AI-based models, ServiceNow has strong business performance and preference from its customers.

data.title: Bread Financial: Loan Growth, Single-Digit P/E, Bullish Chart - Despite Macro Risks
data.description:
Bread Financial Holdings, Inc. (BFH) has trounced its Financials-sector peers recently. Over the past year, shares have returned 58%, while the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) is higher by just 23%, and the Financials sector ETF (XLF) has returned 6%. The short-term lender has also beaten consumer stocks by a wide margin. Indeed, U.S. retail sales data has been decent over the past year, rising 6.2% in the latest Johnson Redbook retail sales report. Macro risks are apparent, of course, but I continue to like Bread.

Today, I reiterate a Buy rating. I had a Buy on BFH back in February of 2024. Shares have returned 105% since then, dividends included. That's about 70 percentage points of alpha to SPY. With a low valuation, solid growth, a new buyback, and a bullish chart, this high-risk, cyclical small-cap has legs higher, in my view.

Bread: A Quiet Financials Leader YoY

Back in January, Bread reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $2.07 topped the Wall Street consensus target of $0.48, while revenue of $975 million, up more than 5% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $20 million beat. Its income from continuing operations increased $45 million during the period, primarily driven by higher net interest income and lower provision for credit losses.

Shares jumped 7.1% in the session that followed, marking a second consecutive post-reporting climb. Looking ahead to the April update, the options market prices in a near-average 6.6% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the report. Shares of the $3.3 billion market cap Financial sector company have elevated implied volatility at 50%, while short interest is high at 9.8%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, Bread’s double beat came with impressive full-year numbers. The company tallied net income of $521 million from continuing operations for the FY 2025, with adjusted diluted EPS rising significantly to $12.16 from $7.74 in 2024.

Operational efficiency and cost discipline were particularly encouraging—Bread's adjusted total non-interest expenses decreased by 1% despite a slight decline in average loans. The firm also improved its credit quality, with its net loss rate falling to 7.7% from 8.2% the previous year.

Color on Q4

During 2025, Bread expanded its network of partners, including Cricket Wireless and Vivint, and grew direct-to-consumer deposits to $8.5 billion, which now account for 48% of total funding. What’s more, capital management remained a priority, highlighted by the repurchase of 5.7 million shares and a 10% increase in the quarterly cash dividend. Then, just last month, the company announced a $600 million increase to the buyback plan, bringing its total remaining capacity to $765 million.

On the call, the management team noted new signings with Caesar’s and Crypto.com, underscoring the upside revenue and earnings potential despite a shaky consumer backdrop. Executives also anticipate that tax refunds may positively affect consumer spending, but that was a bit of a guess, it sounded like.

Looking ahead to the current year, Bread Financial expects low-single-digit growth in both average loans and revenue, while continuing to focus on AI adoption and further improvements in net loss rates, projected to be between 7.2% and 7.4%.

FY 2026 Outlook

On the earnings outlook, despite recent volatility, Bread should maintain $10-plus operating EPS figures in the years ahead. FY 2026 may see a 10% drop, but out-year estimates are more constructive. Top line growth is steadier, in the low-mid single digits.

The sell side, meanwhile, has turned more upbeat on Bread, as evidenced by a high 10 EPS upgrades in the past 90 days, compared to just a lone downgrade. Profitability trends are also healthy right now, while credit performance improved in January.

Bread Financial: Earnings, Valuation, Revisions

On valuation, I have key updates to my previous analysis from more than two years ago. If we assume $10.40 of forward non-GAAP EPS and apply a 9x multiple, then shares should trade near $94. That leaves BFH about $20 to the cheap side today.

I assert that a high-single-digit earnings multiple is appropriate given the current mid-teens-plus EPS growth rate looking ahead to FY 2027 and 2028. Considering the cost discipline, execution prowess, and shareholder-friendly policies, a single-digit earnings multiple is conservative, in my view.

Bread: Very Low P/E and P/B

Key risks for Bread include questionable long-term loan growth potential, given rising competition and emerging credit quality pressure. Investors should monitor monthly credit card delinquency data for clues on how Bread’s book may materialize in the future.

Of course, a macroeconomic downturn would be sharply negative for a cyclical and consumer company like Bread. Loss of key retail partners would be another major headwind if we begin to see that, along with adverse regulatory changes.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q1 2026 earnings date of Thursday, April 23 BMO. Shares pay a $0.23 quarterly dividend on Monday, March 16.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares significantly undervalued, BFH’s technical situation is bullish. Notice in the chart below that the stock is currently in a symmetrical triangle, with a potential apex coming about near $75. Investors should monitor the pattern, but the presumption is that BFH will break out in the trend of larger degree (higher). What’s more, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is likewise coiling—a breakout or breakdown there could be a telltale sign of what price does.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It's on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. Now, with key long-term support near $68 and a high amount of volume by price below that, there should be ample cushion if we see another 10% pullback.

In terms of upside potential, the breakout above $68 triggered an upside measured move price objective to $98, based on the depth of the late 2024 to April decline and subsequent breakout.

BFH: Bullish Consolidation After Last Year's Breakout, $98 Target

The Bottom Line

I have a Buy rating on Bread. This small-cap micro lender sports a low P/E valuation, strong technicals, and a proven management team. Yes, the macro risks are many, but price action and fundamental growth appear solid.

data.title: Fulgent Genetics: A Major Market Misconception
data.description:
Introduction

After the Q4 2025 earnings report, Fulgent Genetics stock suffered a momentous single-day decline of almost 40%. The impetus for this decline was perhaps multi-pronged, but most notably, FLGT announced the in-process departure of their single largest customer, which accounted for $70.8 million, or 22% of total revenue in 2025. This put pressure on FLGT's gross margins and in turn their overall profitability, resulting in guidance for a non-GAAP loss of ($1.45) per share for full year 2026. FLGT also guided for a total year-end liquidity position of $685 million, down from roughly $800 million at the end of 2025.

While the earnings report was certainly not FLGT's best, the market, from wrongfully valuing FLGT before the announcement as a speculative pharma-play at best, and a COVID has-been at worst, is now stretching FLGT's valuation even further and ultimately paying investors to acquire the company's shares. The purpose of this article is to take a step back and view the latest developments at the company with a calm and rational eye, and in doing so, show that the current valuation, despite the blemishes, is probably misaligned with the fundamental realities of the business.

Valuation

So, let's start with FLGT's valuation. At the time of this writing, FLGT is trading for just over $14.50 per share with a market cap of just over $450 million. At the same time, FLGT's book value is over $35 per share, meaning its P/B ratio is a mere 0.40. Of course, book value includes intangible assets which are not the most reliable when it comes to conservative valuation. Stripping out all intangible assets, therefore, gives us a tangible book value of about $30, or a price to tangible book ratio of still less than 0.50. But let's go further and remove the entire PP&E line, giving us an NCAV (current assets + liquid long-term investments) of $26 per share and a P/NCAV of 0.56.

If we remove operating assets entirely, at year end 2025, FLGT had a net liquidity position (cash/equivalents/marketable securities) of $811 million (note: this number assumes tax returns of $106 million from tax credits purchased during the year). Subtracting total liabilities and dividing by shares outstanding of 31.7 million gives us a net liquidity position of over $22 per share. If we do the same calculation but use the guided cash figure of $685 million for year-end 2026, we still get a net liquidity position of $18.74. This gives us price/liquidity ratios of 0.66 and 0.77, respectively. These exercises are summarized in the below table, with valuation metrics ordered from least conservative to most.

Metric Ratio Notes/Adjustments
Price/Book 0.40 N/A
Price/Tangible Book 0.50 N/A
Price/NCAV 0.56 (current assets + long term investments) - total liabilities
Price/Liquidity (EoY 2025) 0.66 Assuming receipt of $106 million of tax credits
Price/Liquidity (EoY 2026) 0.77 Based on management guidance during 2025 Q4 earnings call

When we consider this in the hypothetical context of purchasing Fulgent outright as a private business, the potential absurdity of FLGT's valuation becomes apparent. FLGT is currently trading at a negative enterprise value, or its net liquidity position exceeds its entire market cap. This means that not only is the market giving away FLGT's core operating assets for free, but it is theoretically paying investors to take ownership of FLGT's shares.

This can also be illustrated through the concept of reproduction value popularized by Bruce Greenwald in his book Value Investing: From Graham to Buffett and Beyond. Reproduction value, put simply, values companies based on the economic cost it would require for a competitor to reproduce the businesses' operations. This value that does not always appear directly on the balance sheet. In FLGT's case, not only is the market paying no heed to the reproduction value of its tangible assets like working capital and PP&E, but it is also ignoring its less-than-tangible assets such as research and development spend (especially for Fulgent Pharma), sales and marketing infrastructure, customer relations, brand recognition, and, most recently, acquisition value. Deriving a reproduction valuation figure for these assets is possible, but in the case of FLGT it is likely superfluous, as the entirety of FLGT's reproduction value can be treated as a free (or less than free) call option for the investor.

Risks

The most prominent risk for any investment based on balance sheet value is the potential for cash burn. This very concept might go a long way in explaining why FLGT trades so cheaply. In my last article I discussed how Fulgent Genetics acquired Fulgent Pharma in 2022, in an effort to establish itself as a "one-stop-shop" health care services business. While this sounds exciting, pharmaceutical companies are notorious for spending large amounts of money on research and development to develop new drug candidates and push them through the FDA's multi-staged approval process. This, of course, typically results in tremendous amounts of cash burn, making an investment in a pharmaceutical company inherently risky despite it possessing what might appear to be a fortress-like balance sheet, and the market is not hesitant to value it as such.

FLGT probably does not belong in this category of speculative pharmaceuticals for two reasons. First is its use of the FDA's 505(b)(2) process for its two drug candidates FID-007 and FID-022. The 505(b)(2) process is used as an abbreviated pathway for companies to get FDA approval for modified versions of existing drugs, unlike the significantly more expensive and time-intensive 505(b)(1) path used by most pharmaceutical companies to develop new drug candidates from scratch. FLGT is using the 505(b)(2) path to improve on the delivery of existing drugs Paclitaxel (FID-007) and Irinotecan (FID-022) through its proprietary nanoencapsulation technology. The market may not realize this yet and is unfairly assigning a valuation to FLGT as though it is a classic pharmaceutical firm prone to burn through its vast hoard of cash.

The second reason the market might be wrong about FLGT's valuation is the expectation that FLGT will need to rely on outside capital to fund its pharmaceutical ventures. This is not the case. Instead, Fulgent Pharma is being largely funded through interest income from the company's large collection of marketable securities and short-term investments, and an increasingly profitable lab services business. To illustrate this, let's dive into FLGT's latest earnings report for FY 2025 and establish an estimate of cash flow from the company's core operations.

FY 2025 Earnings and Core Cash Flows

FLGT's headline profitability figures were not flattering. At a glance, FLGT's full year net income and operating cash flow numbers were -$60 million and -$102 million, respectively. We can see the reconciliation of FLGT's GAAP net loss below:

As our valuation of FLGT's shares is based on the company's liquidity position, the cash flow statement is more relevant to us for discerning the company's propensity for cash burn and margin of safety erosion. From this loss of roughly $60 million on the income statement, let's see how this translates to cash flow from operations after adjusting for non-cash items. The company's reconciliation of this can be seen below.

Let's start with operating cash flow and adjust the various line items to arrive at a number that we believe is more representative of the company's normalized cash from operations. For the sake of this exercise, we will only make adjustments for the items that have a meaningful impact to the reconciliation of operating cash flows. The most glaring of these items is the 'Income tax' line under the 'Changes in operating assets and liabilities' section. In the 2025 10-k, management discloses that the "decrease in cash provided from operating activities in 2025 compared to the corresponding 2024 period was primarily due to the purchase of Investment Tax Credits for $99.6 million in cash in 2025." Further, the company expects to receive $106 million in 2026 as a result of these purchases. Therefore, from an operational cash burn perspective, it is reasonable to add this line back to the overall cash flow from operations, giving us a positive adjusted cash flow from operations of roughly $3 million.

The next item on the cash flow statement to analyze is share based compensation. While this is not technically a cash expense, it does result in share dilution, which has implications to our liquidity per share calculation from above. On the cash flow statement, SBC is listed at just under $40 million. It is important to note, however, that this figure is derived from the grant price of the RSUs that vested during FY 2025. In FLGT's case, most of the RSUs that vested during 2025 were granted at much higher prices from years previous, meaning the accounting expense on FLGT's books may actually be inflated. Looking at the company's statement of stockholder's equity in the 2025 10-k, we can see that 1.049 million RSUs vested during the period.

Taking this figure and multiplying it by the average share price in 2025 of $20.83 gives us a more accurate SBC expense of $21.85 million. Subtracting this from our $3 million figure above gives us cash burn of -$18.85 million for the year.

If we want to break this down further and attempt to identify cash flow specific to FLGT's core lab services business, we can add back the roughly $25 million of cash that was used to further FLGT's pharmaceutical endeavors. This puts us at cash flow from operations of about $6 million for the core lab services segment. There are a few things we need to consider here, however. Firstly, this is not a precise figure. Rather, it is used to illustrate the fact that FLGT is not a typical pharmaceutical firm speculatively burning cash on drug discovery. Indeed, FLGT, while not entirely devoid of cash burn, has a core lab services business that, combined with interest income from the company's marketable securities, has at least break-even cash flow.

We should also be cognizant of the fact that the fourth quarter of 2025 was challenging for FLGT, which impacted its full year results. As alluded to above, FLGT's largest customer, taking up 22% of total revenue, began to transition their testing in-house. With smaller revenue volume allocated across the same amount of fixed costs, FLGT's gross margins suffered. This, combined with higher operational expenses from acquisition-related costs throughout the year, might cause FLGT's profit margins to be less robust than they would be in a longer term, more normalized environment. This impact is expected to carry over into 2026 as well, hence the disappointing guidance of a loss of ($1.45) per share and drastically decreased liquidity position. However, management opines on this in their earnings commentary:

But kind of like taking a step back and looking at our cash burn, -- we ended the year with approximately $800 million if you include the receivables that we're going to be getting from the IRS tax refund. And now we're forecasting our cash at the end of 2026 to be $685 million. But a huge chunk of that delta of $115 million are costs and a cash outlay that's not associated with the laboratory services business. So for example, of the $115 million, at least $56 million is going to be associated with the cash outlay that we have for the Bako acquisition.

We have another $26 million of outlay that's associated with the spend for our biotech asset, the 007 and the 002. We also have capital purchases of approximately $12 million and the onetime professional liability settlement of $14.5 million. So if you kind of like take a step back and even if you take into account the impact of the loss of this customer, our laboratory services business is going to be using cash, but not that much, which leaves a lot of cash for us to deploy for M&A investment in our overall business as well as other opportunities that can serve the shareholders.

A Word on Buybacks

Something that may re-instill confidence in FLGT's investor base is the resumption of their share repurchase program. The company originally announced their stock buyback program in March of 2022. The original amount of total authorized buybacks was $250 million. As of the most recent earnings report, there are approximately $140 million left to be deployed as part of the program.

Let's use a hypothetical scenario to see just how much value creation can be achieved through aggressive buybacks at these levels. Assuming, for argument's sake, that the company were to deploy the entirety of their remaining buyback funds today for a price of $14.50 per share, the valuation table that we built above would look like the following.

Metric Ratio Before Buybacks Ratio After Buybacks

Price/Book

 0.40 0.29
Price/Tangible Book 0.50 0.34
Price/NCAV 0.56 0.39
Price/Liquidity (EoY 2025) 0.66 0.45
Price/Liquidity (EoY 2026) 0.77 0.54

The practicality of FLGT using the rest of the buyback program instantaneously and acquiring all shares for the same price is irrelevant here. Rather, the point of this exercise is to show that at such low valuation multiples, FLGT has a real opportunity to create value for its shareholders by maximizing share repurchases at these depressed prices.

Conclusion

FLGT should not be valued by the market as a traditional, cash-guzzling pharmaceutical firm. The reason for this is two-fold. Firstly, FLGT is pursuing approval through the FDA's 505(b)(2) pathway. This enables them to drastically reduce spending on research and development and shorten the time it takes to push their drug candidates to market. The second reason is that FLGT, strictly speaking, is not a pharmaceutical firm. The core of its operations is made up of its Lab Services business, which, in conjunction with the interest income from the company's marketable securities, largely funds its pharmaceutical endeavors.

The realization of this market misconception has perhaps been delayed and the price-to-value gap exacerbated by the company's less-than-flattering earnings report. The patient investor should be consoled, however, by the increased margin of safety as a result of the immense share price decline. This increased margin of safety should enable the investor to protect their downside while waiting patiently for a turn in the company's fortunes that could rerate FLGT's shares dramatically to the upside.

data.title: Ambarella: It Will Need To Do A Lot To Get Back Investor Confidence
data.description:
Introduction

Ambarella, Inc. (AMBA) recently reported its Q4 results, which were not met very well by the investors, as its share price dropped by over 19% since the report. I wanted to go over the numbers and give some comments on the outlook, and why I don’t think it is still a good idea to start a position yet, even after this drop.

By the Numbers

Starting from the top, revenues came in at around $101m for Q4, up 20% y/y, and just barely beat estimates by around $730k. Looking at the breakdown in revenues in more detail, IoT came in strong at around 50% y/y growth. This segment includes security cameras, video conferencing, as well as the most promising growth drivers (in my opinion), the robotics/drone businesses. Automotive has been a little sluggish over the last while, unsurprisingly, and this past quarter hasn’t shown much improvement either, growing in the high single digits. At least it was positive, signaling some sort of recovery.

Management has specifically singled out drones and warehouse robotics in the quarter as they continue to be the most promising new ventures.

Looking at the company’s efficiency and profitability, Q4 non-GAAP EPS came in at around 13 cents, beating estimates by 3 cents. Gross margins for Q4 came in at around 58.4% on a GAAP basis versus 60% at the same time last year. For the full year, that number ended up being 59.2% vs. 60.5% last year. So, not good all around. The management attributed the performance to a few things. The customer mix was most likely the largest contributor to the performance. The company has been winning more contracts, especially with high-volume customers, which means that these customers may have greater pricing power and are able to negotiate prices a bit more when it comes to their contracts. But it’s not really an issue, as the management has been explicit regarding the trade for more gross profit dollars that come with lower profit percentages. Additionally, the company has been transitioning its product lineup to 5nm and 4nm chips. This means that the newer nodes are a bit more expensive to manufacture in the early stages, and their manufacturing yields are much lower than those of mature nodes. But since they will eventually command a higher average selling price, the margins will recover.

Going over the company’s financial situation, AMBA finished the year with around $191m in cash and equivalents, as well as $121.5m in marketable securities against no debt, except for some operating lease liabilities. This is a great position to be in, especially when the company is still not GAAP-profitable. AMBA is not burdened by any debt obligations, which means that all the capital resources can be focused on growing the company.

On top of these results, the company gave us a look at the next quarter, with a guidance of $97m-$103m for revenues, as well as a non-GAAP gross margin to be between 59% and 60.5%. Additionally, operating expenses are expected to be $55m-$58m for the next quarter. Analysts were expecting around $97m for revenues, so that was a decent beat on expectations. Margins and OpEx were more or less in line with the consensus.

It seems that the investing community didn’t like the results and the guidance, since on the day of the announcement, AMBA’s share price cratered by as much as 20% at one point and hasn’t recovered since. It is trading close to its 52-week lows, but there is still quite a bit to go before reaching those lows. I thought the results were fine. The Edge AI, or Physical AI as they say, was quite promising, with decent IoT segment growth; however, I understand the reaction. It may seem a bit of an overreaction since I don’t think it deserved to see such a drop in its value, but that shows us that the company was priced to perfection, and anything but stellar results will bring some fear and uncertainty. Even with this drop in the share price, the company’s forward P/E ratio is close to 72x.

Comments on the Outlook

I think the management has to do a lot going forward to restore investor confidence. Even with IoT growing at a whopping 50% y/y, it wasn’t enough to keep them satisfied, so I don’t know what the company has to do to fix this. I believe it will all depend on the company’s profitability at this point. Investors may have gotten a little jittery since AMBA saw some margin deterioration. I don’t think it’s a big problem if the dollar amount is increasing. But on the surface, it does seem to be a big deal to many.

The future advanced products, like the 5nm and 4nm chips, should help the company either maintain the current margins we see today, or we will see some improvement, but that will depend on how the company executes on this strategic shift going forward. Anything AI has a higher price attached, as these hyperscalers seem to have unlimited pockets to keep acquiring the newest and most advanced technology to keep up in the race, so AMBA should see some margin improvements overall. Even in the latest quarter, the new chips were already showing high single-digit percentage growth, and that is still at the early stages of adoption.

The aerial drones segment for the company had a full quarter of production revenue. AMBA’s CEO, Fermi Wang, said that this continues to be one of their highest-volume mobile robotic markets today, and is quite confident that this is going to be a star performer in the future. This market segment has been a speculative part of Ambarella for a very long time, which has more or less changed recently, as the company’s “Antigravity A1” had a breakthrough. AMBA’s CV5 AI SoC is the brain on the Antigravity A1 drone, which is the world’s first 8K 360-degree drone that uses AMBA’s AI to handle partially autonomous flight and difficult-to-process video data.

In the mobile robotics segment, the management mentioned that the company won a significant design win at the warehouses of a large U.S.-based e-commerce provider. This is currently going to be in a low-volume production, but is expected to ramp significantly in 2027.

Should you invest after the recent drop?

I think I would need to see what happens to the company’s profitability profile over the next couple of quarters before I decide it is a good time to start a position. It seems that the company has been priced to perfection, and now all of a sudden, the company isn’t delivering as well as it did before, which brings some fear that costs the company quite a bit, as hypergrowth investors are not satisfied with the way things are going.

I am going to maintain my Hold rating until I see what happens to its profitability in the future, as AMBA’s valuation still seems stretched even after this recent correction.
